summary: Jeff Atwood invites readers to help name his upcoming developer community website, which would later become Stack Overflow. He reflects on the extreme difficulty of choosing a good domain name in an era of Web 2.0 startups and rampant domain squatting. The post presents a poll of potential names, with stackoverflow.com ultimately winning with 25% of the vote. Atwood emphasizes the importance of naming as a crucial early step in building a new product, while acknowledging that finding an available, memorable domain is remarkably challenging.
key_points: Naming a website is extraordinarily difficult, even when consulting experienced developers
The domain name landscape is a 'wasteland' of taken names and domain squatters
Community input can be valuable for major branding decisions
Having a name helps crystallize and communicate what you're building
Even serious companies sometimes end up with unintentionally problematic domain names (like experts-exchange.com)
money_quotes: Naming a website is hard. Really, really hard.
You begin to have a new respect for all the crazily-named Web 2.0 startups.
What were the people who named experts-exchange.com thinking? I'm not so sure they were.
It sure helps to put a name on it first.
I'm warning you: it's a wasteland out there.
You'll have to be pretty clever indeed to come up with an interesting, simple name that isn't taken – or, worse, domain-squatted.
key_insight: Even for experienced developers building what would become one of the most influential programming websites, naming remains one of the hardest and most underappreciated challenges in launching a product.
